title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the evolution of bioluminescence. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Light in the Dark: The Science of Living Glow
content.text: In the pitch-black depths of the midnight zone, nearly five kilometers beneath the ocean surface, the sun is a distant memory. Yet, this world is far from dark. Instead, it is illuminated by a spectacular array of flickering lights, neon flashes, and glowing lures. This is bioluminescence—the ability of living organisms to produce their own light through a chemical reaction. ||(41)|| [41]For most creatures, this light is produced by the interaction between a molecule called luciferin and an enzyme named luciferase[/41]. While the chemistry is relatively consistent, the evolutionary reasons for developing this trait are incredibly diverse. In the deep sea, where 90% of inhabitants are bioluminescent, light is the primary language of survival. ||(42)|| [42]The most famous example of this is the anglerfish[/42]. Hanging from its forehead is a glowing 'fishing pole' filled with symbiotic bacteria. In the darkness, unsuspecting prey are drawn to the light like moths to a flame, only to find themselves trapped in a mouth of needle-like teeth. ||(43)|| [43]However, light can also be a shield[/43]. Many species of squid and shrimp use bioluminescence for 'counter-illumination.' By matching the faint light filtering down from the surface, they can erase their own shadows, making them invisible to predators lurking below. It is a biological form of a 'cloaking device.' ||(44)|| [44]Beyond the ocean, bioluminescence is rarer but no less impressive[/44]. Fireflies are perhaps the most recognizable examples, using rhythmic flashes to find mates in the summer twilight. Each species has its own unique 'Morse code' to ensure they are communicating with the right partners. ||(45)|| [45]This discovery has led to a revolution in medical research[/45]. By tagging specific genes with bioluminescent markers, scientists can now track the spread of cancer cells or the effectiveness of new drugs in real-time. The 'glow' has become a powerful tool in the fight against disease. ||(46)|| [46]As we continue to explore the deepest trenches of our planet, we find that bioluminescence is not just a quirky biological trick[/46]. It is a fundamental strategy for life in the shadows, a testament to the ingenuity of evolution in the face of total darkness.

paragraphs.B: This [41] cold light is remarkably efficient [/41]. Unlike an incandescent bulb, which loses 90% of its energy as heat, bioluminescence converts nearly all its energy into illumination. This efficiency is vital for organisms living in environments where every calorie of energy must be carefully conserved.
paragraphs.C: One of [42] the primary uses of this ability is predation [/42]. In a world with no landmarks, a well-placed light can act as a beacon, luring curious organisms directly into the path of a hungry predator.
paragraphs.D: But [43] the use of light isn't always about luring food [/43]. Sometimes, it serves as a startling deterrent. Certain jellyfish release a cloud of glowing 'bombs' when attacked, confusing the predator and allowing the jellyfish to drift away into the safety of the dark.
paragraphs.G: This [44] mastery of light is not restricted to the sea [/44]. On land, various fungi and insects have evolved similar capabilities, though often for very different ecological reasons, such as attracting insects to spread their spores.
paragraphs.A: Researchers have even [45] managed to isolate the proteins responsible [/45] for these terrestrial glows. The Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP), originally found in jellyfish, is now used in labs across the world to illuminate the microscopic processes within living cells.
paragraphs.F: This [46] transition from natural wonder to scientific utility [/46] highlights how much we can learn from the natural world. What started as an observation of deep-sea curiosities has ended up saving countless human lives through better diagnostic imaging.
paragraphs.E: Many [47] people mistake bioluminescence for fluorescence [/47]. While they both involve light, fluorescence requires an external light source to be absorbed and re-emitted, whereas bioluminescence is a self-contained chemical reaction that occurs entirely within the organism.

questions.explanation: B explains the efficiency of 'this cold light', following the general definition of bioluminescence in the opening paragraph.
C introduces 'predation' as a use for light, which provides the perfect lead-in for the 'anglerfish' example described in the next paragraph.
D transitions from 'luring food' to other uses of light, which fits between the predatory anglerfish and the defensive 'cloaking device' of the squid.
G notes that 'mastery of light is not restricted to the sea', which connects the deep-sea section to the firefly and terrestrial examples that follow.
A mentions the isolation of proteins like GFP, which explains the 'discovery' and 'medical research' applications mentioned after the gap.
F summarizes the 'transition from natural wonder to scientific utility', echoing the medical and tracking uses described before the gap.
